title: Reflect points
description: Given a point and a definition of a horizontal or vertical reflection, plot the reflection on a coordinate plane or identify the coordinates of the reflected point.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Plot the image of point $A$ under a reflection across line $\ell$.**  

[[☃ interactive-graph 1]]
hints:
A reflection moves a point perpendicularly across a line, so that the [[☃ definition 1]] is the same distance from the line as the [[☃ definition 2]], but on the other side.



[[☃ image 1]]

In other words, the line of reflection is the perpendicular bisector between any pair of corresponding points in the image and the pre-image.
Point $A$ is $4$ units directly to the right of line $\ell$, so $A'$ will be $4$ units to the left of the line and have the same $y$-coordinate as $A$.

This is the image of point $A$:  



[[☃ image 1]]

Point $B$ is $6$ units directly above line $m$, so $B'$ will be $6$ units below the line and have the same $x$-coordinate as $B$.

This is the image of point $B$:

assessment_items.question_content:
**Plot the image of point $A$ under a reflection across the $y$-axis.**  

[[☃ interactive-graph 1]]
hints:
A reflection moves a point perpendicularly across a line, so that the [[☃ definition 1]] is the same distance from the line as the [[☃ definition 2]], but on the other side.



[[☃ image 1]]

In other words, the line of reflection is the perpendicular bisector between any pair of corresponding points in the image and the pre-image.
Point $A$ is $2$ units directly to the right of the $y$-axis, so $A'$ will be $2$ units to the left of the $y$-axis and have the same $y$-coordinate as $A$.
The image of point $A$ is $(-2,5)$.  



[[☃ image 1]]

assessment_items.question_content:
Point $Q'$ is the image of $Q(3, -4)$ under a reflection across the $x$-axis.

**What are the coordinates of $Q'$?**

$($ [[☃ numeric-input 1]] $,$ [[☃ numeric-input 2]] $)$
hints:
A reflection moves a point perpendicularly across a line, so that the [[☃ definition 1]] is the same distance from the line as the [[☃ definition 2]], but on the other side.



[[☃ image 1]]

In other words, the line of reflection is the perpendicular bisector between any pair of corresponding points in the image and the pre-image.

We want to reflect $Q(3,-4)$ across the $x$-axis.  



[[☃ image 1]]

Point $Q$ is $4$ units directly below the $x$-axis, so $Q'$ will be $4$ units above the axis and have the same $x$-coordinate as $Q$.

Algebraically, this means a reflection across the $x$-axis is the transformation $(x,y) \to (x,-y)$.

$\begin{align}
Q &\to Q'
\\\\
(x,y) &\to (x,-y)
\\\\
(3, -4) &\to (3,-(-4))
\end{align}$

**The coordinates of $Q'$ are $(3, 4)$.**

This is how it looks graphically:



[[☃ image 1]]

assessment_items.question_content:
Point $D'$ is the image of $D(-2, 1)$ under a reflection across the $x$-axis.

**What are the coordinates of $D'$?**

$($ [[☃ numeric-input 1]] $,$ [[☃ numeric-input 2]] $)$
hints:
We want to reflect $D(-2, 1)$ across the $x$-axis.  



[[☃ image 1]]

Point $D$ is $1$ units directly above the $x$-axis, so $D'$ will be $1$ units below the axis and have the same $x$-coordinate as $D$.

Algebraically, this means a reflection across the $x$-axis is the transformation $(x,y) \to (x,-y)$.

A reflection moves a point perpendicularly across a line, so that the [[☃ definition 1]] is the same distance from the line as the [[☃ definition 2]], but on the other side.



[[☃ image 1]]

In other words, the line of reflection is the perpendicular bisector between any pair of corresponding points in the image and the pre-image.

$\begin{align}
D &\to D'
\\\\
(x,y) &\to (x,-y)
\\\\
(-2, 1) &\to (-2,-(1))
\end{align}$

**The coordinates of $D'$ are $(-2, -1)$.**

This is how it looks graphically:  



[[☃ image 1]]

assessment_items.question_content:
Point $C'$ is the image of $C(-4, -2)$ under a reflection across the $y$-axis.

**What are the coordinates of $C'$?**

$($ [[☃ numeric-input 1]] $,$ [[☃ numeric-input 2]] $)$
hints:
A reflection moves a point perpendicularly across a line, so that the [[☃ definition 1]] is the same distance from the line as the [[☃ definition 2]], but on the other side.



[[☃ image 1]]

In other words, the line of reflection is the perpendicular bisector between any pair of corresponding points in the image and the pre-image.

We want to reflect $C(-4, -2)$ across the $y$-axis.  



[[☃ image 1]]

Point $C$ is $4$ units directly to the left of the $y$-axis, so $C'$ will be $4$ units to the right of the axis and have the same $y$-coordinate as $C$.

Algebraically, this means a reflection across the $y$-axis is the transformation $(x,y) \to (-x,y)$.

**The coordinates of $C'$ are $(4, -2)$.**

This is how it looks graphically:  



[[☃ image 1]]

assessment_items.question_content:
Point $P'$ is the image of $P(3, 5)$ under a reflection across the $y$-axis.

**What are the coordinates of $P'$?**

$($ [[☃ numeric-input 1]] $,$ [[☃ numeric-input 2]] $)$
hints:
A reflection moves a point perpendicularly across a line, so that the [[☃ definition 1]] is the same distance from the line as the [[☃ definition 2]], but on the other side.



[[☃ image 1]]

In other words, the line of reflection is the perpendicular bisector between any pair of corresponding points in the image and the pre-image.

We want to reflect $P(3, 5)$ across the $y$-axis.  



[[☃ image 1]]

Point $P$ is $3$ units directly to the right of the $y$-axis, so $P'$ will be $3$ units to the left of the axis and have the same $y$-coordinate as $P$.

Algebraically, this means a reflection across the $y$-axis is the transformation $(x,y) \to (-x,y)$.

$\begin{align}
P &\to P'
\\\\
(x,y) &\to (-x,y)
\\\\
(3, 5) &\to (-(3),5)
\end{align}$

**The coordinates of $P'$ are $(-3, 5)$.**

This is how it looks graphically:  



[[☃ image 1]]
